figures.caption: Fig. 2. The generic Multi-Agent LLM layer, instantiated once per generative phase (Phases 2–5), as described in the text.
Fig. 3. The state machine of the Last Response Engine modelled using RoboChart [36].

Table 3. Pre-study background survey responses from the six participants (P1–P6); each item was rated on a four-point experience scale: 1 = novice (0–6 months), 2 = intermediate (6 months–1 year), 3 = experienced (1–3 years), 4 = expert (>3 years).

Fig. 4. Per-stage Single- vs. Multi-agent scores per model (mean over ten repetitions). Stars mark significant differences in either direction (Mann–Whitney U for continuous F1; Fisher’s exact for binary rates): 𝑝 < 0.05: *, 𝑝 < 0.01: **, 𝑝 < 0.001: ***, 𝑝 < 0.0001: ****. Bar heights show direction; a “0” is a measured zero (e.g. Sonnet’s 0/10 EOL runs), not missing data.

Fig. 1. Overview of RADIANT and its realisation in our tool MALCOM (Section 3.3). The middle (LLM) lane produces JSON/source artefacts per phase via multi-agent generation; the bottom (MDE) lane transforms each into models and Bifrost traceability. Only the requirement model (Phase 1, dashed) is authored manually; all other artefacts are generated.

Table 4. Comparative experiment for efficiency evaluation. All participants start from the same given requirement model (the input to RADIANT); the automated setting fixes the pipeline to DeepSeek-V4.
